# Page 1^page=1
Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition, Chapter 11: File System Implementation

# Page 2^page=2
11.2 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition Chapter 11: File System Implementation u File-System Structure u File-System Implementation u Directory Implementation u Allocation Methods u Free-Space Management u Efficiency and Performance u Recovery u NFS u Example: WAFL File System

# Page 3^page=3
11.3 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition Objectives uTo describe the details of implementing local file systems and directory structures uTo describe the implementation of remote file systems uTo discuss block allocation and free-block algorithms and trade-offs

# Page 4^page=4
11.4 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition File-System Structure u File structure Ø Logical storage unit; Collection of related information u File system organized into layers u File system resides on secondary storage (disks) Ø Provides efficient and convenient access to disk by allowing data to be stored, located retrieved easily u File control block – storage structure consisting of information about a file u Device driver controls the physical device

# Page 5^page=5
11.5 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition Layered File System drivers、Interrupt handlers Drive Cylinder Track sector FCB

# Page 6^page=6
11.6 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition File-System Implementation u Boot control block contains info needed by system to boot OS from that volume u Volume control block contains volume details u Directory structure organizes the files u Per-file File Control Block (FCB) contains many details about the file A Typical File Control Block

# Page 7^page=7
11.7 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition Implementing File Operations u Create a file: Ø Find space in the file system, add directory entry. u Open file Ø System call specifying name of file. system searches directory structure to find file. Ø 根据文件号查系统打开文件表，看文件是否已被打开； ü 是→共享计数加1 ü 否→将外存中的FCB等信息填入系统打开文件表空表项，共享计数置为1； Ø 在用户打开文件表中取一空表项，填写打开方式等，并指向系统打开文件表 对应表项 Ø System keeps current file position pointer to the location where next write/read occurs Ø System call returns file descriptor (a handle) to user process u Writing in a file: Ø System call specifying file descriptor and information to be written Ø Writes information at location pointed by the files current pointer

# Page 8^page=8
11.8 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition Implementing File Operations u Reading a file: Ø System call specifying file descriptor and number of bytes to read (and possibly where in memory to stick contents). u Repositioning within a file: Ø System call specifying file descriptor and new location of current pointer Ø (also called a file seek even though does not interact with disk) u Closing a file: Ø System call specifying file descriptor Ø Call removes current file position pointer and file descriptor associated with process and file(打开文件表) Ø 若在文件打开期间，该文件作过某种修改，则应将其写回到辅存。 u Deleting a file: Ø Search directory structure for named file, release associated file space and erase directory entry u Truncating a file: Ø Keep attributes the same, but reset file size to 0, and reclaim file space.

# Page 9^page=9
11.9 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition 9 Multiple users of a file u OS typically keeps two levels of internal tables: u Per-process table Ø Information about the use of the file by the user (e.g. current file position pointer) u System wide table Ø Gets created by first process which opens the file Ø Location of file on disk Ø Access dates Ø File size Ø Count of how many processes have the file open (used for deletion)

# Page 10^page=10
11.10 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition In-Memory File System Structures

# Page 11^page=11
11.11 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition Virtual File Systems u Virtual File Systems (VFS) provide an object-oriented way of implementing file systems.VFS allows the same system call interface (the API) to be used for different types of file systems. u The API is to the VFS interface, rather than any specific type of file system. Schematic View of Virtual File System

# Page 12^page=12
11.12 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition 12 File System Layout u File System is stored on disks Ø Disk is divided into 1 or more partitions Ø Sector 0 of disk called Master Boot Record Ø End of MBR has partition table (start & end address of partitions) u First block of each partition has boot block Ø Loaded by MBR and executed on boot

# Page 13^page=13
11.13 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition 13 Storing Files Files can be allocated in different ways: u Contiguous allocation Ø All bytes together, in order u Linked Structure Ø Each block points to the next block u Indexed Structure Ø An index block contains pointer to many other blocks Rhetorical Questions -- which is best? Ø For sequential access? Random access? Ø Large files? Small files? Mixed?

# Page 14^page=14
11.14 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition 14 Implementing Files u Contiguous Allocation: allocate files contiguously on disk u Each file occupies a set of contiguous blocks on the disk; u Simple – only starting location (block #) and length (number of blocks) are required; Random access; u Wasteful of space (dynamic storage-allocation problem);Files cannot grow;

# Page 15^page=15
11.15 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition Contiguous Allocation u Mapping from logical to physical LA/512 Q块 R位移 Block to be accessed = Q + starting address Displacement into block = R Contiguous Allocation of Disk Space

# Page 16^page=16
11.16 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition 16 Contiguous Allocation u Pros: Ø Simple: state required per file is start block and size Ø Performance: entire file can be read with one seek u Cons: Ø Fragmentation: external is bigger problem Ø Usability: user needs to know size of file u Used in CDROMs, DVDs

# Page 17^page=17
11.17 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition Extent-Based Systems u Many newer file systems (I.e. Veritas File System) use a modified contiguous allocation scheme u Extent-based file systems allocate disk blocks in extents u An extent is a contiguous block of disks Ø Extents are allocated for file allocation Ø A file consists of one or more extents

# Page 18^page=18
11.18 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition Linked Allocation u Simple – need only starting address u Free-space management system – no waste of space u No random access u Mapping uBlock to be accessed is the Qth block in the linked chain of blocks representing the file. uDisplacement into block = R + 1(指针占一字节) LA/511 Q R u Each file is a linked list of disk blocks: blocks may be scattered anywhere on the disk. pointerblock =

# Page 19^page=19
11.19 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition Linked Allocation u Pros: Ø No space lost to external fragmentation Ø Disk only needs to maintain first block of each file u Cons: Ø Random access is costly Ø Overheads of pointers

# Page 20^page=20
11.20 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition 20 MS-DOS(OS/2) File System u Implement a linked list allocation using a table Ø Called File Allocation Table (FAT) Ø Take pointer away from blocks, store in this table Ø Can cache FAT in-memory File-Allocation Table

# Page 21^page=21
11.21 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition 21 FAT Discussion u Pros: Ø Entire block is available for data Ø Random access is faster since entire FAT is in memory u Cons: Ø Entire FAT should be in memory l For 20 GB disk, 1 KB block size, FAT has 20 million entries l If 4 bytes used per entry Þ 80 MB of main memory required for FS

# Page 22^page=22
11.22 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition Indexed Allocation u Brings all pointers together into the index block u Logical view index table Example

# Page 23^page=23
11.23 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition Indexed Allocation (Cont.) u Need index table u Random access u Dynamic access without external fragmentation, but have overhead of index block u Mapping from logical to physical in a file of maximum size of 256K words and block size of 512 words. We need only 1 block for index table LA/512 Q R Q = displacement into index table R = displacement into block

# Page 24^page=24
11.24 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition Indexed Allocation – Mapping (Cont.) u Mapping from logical to physical in a file of unbounded length (block size of 512 words) 扩充的实现方案如下: 1. Linked scheme – Link blocks of index table (no limit on size) LA / (512 x 511) Q1 R1 Q1 = block of index table R1 is used as follows: R1 / 512 Q2 R2 Q2 = displacement into block of index table R2 displacement into block of file:

# Page 25^page=25
11.25 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition Indexed Allocation – Mapping (Cont.) 2. Multilevel (for exam. Two-level index)index (maximum file size is 5123) LA / (512 x 512) Q1 R1 Q1 = displacement into outer-index R1 is used as follows: R1 / 512 Q2 R2 Q2 = displacement into block of index table R2 displacement into block of file:  outer-index index table file

# Page 26^page=26
11.26 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition 3. Combined Scheme: UNIX UFS (4K bytes per block) 共15个指针项，前 12个是直接指针 u If data blocks are 4K … Ø First 48K reachable from the inode Ø Next 4MB available from single-indirect Ø Next 4GB available from double-indirect Ø Next 4TB available through the triple-indirect block u Any block can be found with at most 3 disk accesses

# Page 27^page=27
11.27 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition Questions? u Performance? u Efficiency? Ø For sequential access? Random access? Ø Large files? Small files? Mixed? Ø HDD VS. SSD?

# Page 28^page=28
11.28 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition 28 Managing Free Disk Space 2 approaches to keep track of free disk blocks u Linked list and bitmap approach

# Page 29^page=29
11.29 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition Free-Space Management u Bit vector(bitmap) (n blocks) … 0 1 2 n-1 bit[i] =0 Þ block[i] free 1 Þ block[i] occupied Block number calculation (number of bits per word) * (number of 0-value words) + offset of first 1 bit u Bit map requires extra space Ø Example: u block size = 212 bytes u disk size = 230 bytes (1 gigabyte) u n = 230/212 = 218 bits (or 32K bytes) u Easy to get contiguous files

# Page 30^page=30
11.30 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition Linked Free Space List on Disk uLinked list (free list) ØCannot get contiguous space easily ØNo waste of space 空闲链的改进方法： uGrouping（空闲块按组存放） uCounting（利用连续性：只记录第一 个块地址和后续连续的空闲快个数）

# Page 31^page=31
11.31 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition UNIX的成组链接法 u 空闲盘块号栈：存放当前可用的空闲盘块号及空闲盘块号数N（最多100个）;文 件区的所有空闲盘块被分成若干组; u 第一组盘块总数和盘块号记入空闲盘块号栈中;每一组的第一个盘块中记录有下 一组的盘块总数和盘块号

# Page 32^page=32
11.32 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition 32 u When a file is opened, OS uses path name to find dir Ø Directory has information about the file’s disk blocks • Whole file (contiguous), first block (linked-list) or I-node Ø Directory also has attributes of each file u Directory: map ASCII file name to file attributes & location u 2 options: entries have all attributes, or point to file I-node Directory Implementation

# Page 33^page=33
11.33 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition UNIX采用(b),给定文件路径名为／usr／ast／mbox，检索过程如下: 1 1 4 7 14 9 6 8 . .. bin dev lib etc usr tmp 132 6 1 19 30 51 26 45 . .. disk erik jim ast bat 406 26 6 64 92 60 81 17 . .. grants books mbox minix src 根目录 结点6是 ／usr的目录 132#块是/usr 的目录 结点26是 /usr/ast 目录 406#块是 /usr/ast的 目录

# Page 34^page=34
11.34 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition Directory Implementation u Linear list of file names with pointer to the data blocks Ø simple to program Ø time-consuming to execute u Hash Table – linear list with hash data structure Ø decreases directory search time Ø collisions – situations where two file names hash to the same location Ø fixed size

# Page 35^page=35
11.35 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition 35 Managing file names: Example

# Page 36^page=36
11.36 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition Efficiency and Performance u Efficiency dependent on: Ø disk allocation and directory algorithms Ø types of data kept in file’s directory entry u Performance Ø disk cache – separate section of main memory for frequently used blocks Ø free-behind and read-ahead – techniques to optimize sequential access（预先读/延迟写） Ø improve PC performance by dedicating section of memory as virtual disk, or RAM disk

# Page 37^page=37
11.37 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition Page Cache u A page cache caches pages rather than disk blocks using virtual memory techniques u Memory-mapped I/O uses a page cache u Routine I/O through the file system uses the buffer (disk) cache u This leads to the following figure： I/O Without a Unified Buffer Cache I/O Using a Unified Buffer Cache

# Page 38^page=38
11.38 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition Recovery u Consistency checking – compares data in directory structure with data blocks on disk, and tries to fix inconsistencies u Use system programs to back up data from disk to another storage device (magnetic tape, other magnetic disk, optical) u Recover lost file or disk by restoring data from backup

# Page 39^page=39
11.39 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition Log Structured File Systems u Log structured (or journaling) file systems record each update to the file system as a transaction u All transactions are written to a log Ø A transaction is considered committed once it is written to the log Ø However, the file system may not yet be updated u The transactions in the log are asynchronously written to the file system Ø When the file system is modified, the transaction is removed from the log u If the file system crashes, all remaining transactions in the log must still be performed u Example. BEGIN_TRANSACTION; x = x + 1; y = y + 2 x = y * y; END_TRANSACTION; Log [x = 0 / 1] [y = 0/2] [x = 1/4] 写完后系统崩溃

# Page 40^page=40
11.40 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition 40 Network File System (NFS) u Developed by Sun Microsystems in 1984 Ø Used to join FSes on multiple computers as one logical whole u Used commonly today with UNIX systems u Assumptions Ø Allows arbitrary collection of users to share a file system Ø Clients and servers might be on different LANs Ø Machines can be clients and servers at the same time u Architecture: Ø A server exports one or more of its directories to remote clients Ø Clients access exported directories by mounting them u The contents are then accessed as if they were local

# Page 41^page=41
11.41 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition 41 Example

# Page 42^page=42
11.42 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition Three Independent File Systems Mounts Cascading mounts Mounting in NFS

11.43 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition Schematic View of NFS Architecture
